article_title: PatchMon V2 Finally Lets You Patch Your Servers From One Dashboard
core_thesis: PatchMon V2 transforms from a read-only patch monitoring dashboard into a full patch management tool, enabling users to discover, schedule, and apply updates across Linux, FreeBSD, and Windows servers from a single, self-hosted, open-source interface. This addresses the critical workflow gap in v1 where visibility existed but action required separate tools.
detailed_summary: PatchMon V2 is a significant upgrade over its v1 release, which only provided visibility into available updates without the ability to apply them. The new version allows administrators to initiate patching directly from the dashboard, supporting Linux, FreeBSD, and Windows (Windows currently visibility-only, with patching expected in a future release). The installation is streamlined via a Docker Compose setup script that configures reverse proxy options and CORS. After deployment, a setup wizard guides users through admin account creation, MFA configuration, and server URL confirmation. Hosts are added by selecting the OS type, entering details, and running a generated onboarding script on the target machine. Once onboarded, users can patch individual hosts immediately or via policies that define schedules and host group assignments. The interface provides real-time shell output during patching. Policies allow automation by setting patch delays and assigning host groups (managed under Settings, which the author notes is unintuitive). The solution is free and open-source, making it suitable for home labs and production environments.

entities.name: PatchMon
entities.role: Developer of the open-source patch management tool PatchMon V2

entities.name: Brandon Lee
entities.role: Author and reviewer, Senior Writer at Virtualization Howto

entities.name: Virtualization Howto
entities.role: Technology publication covering virtualization, DevOps, and home lab topics

key_data_points: PatchMon V2 adds the ability to actively patch servers (Linux, FreeBSD, Windows) from the dashboard, overcoming v1's limitation of visibility-only. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: centralises patch management, reducing manual SSH/Ansible overhead.)

Installation uses a single Docker Compose command: 'mkdir patchmon && cd patchmon && bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PatchMon/PatchMon/refs/heads/main/docker/setup-env.sh)" && docker compose up -d'. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: quick deployment with minimal configuration.)

Windows support is currently visibility-only; actual patching for Windows is planned for a future release. (🟢 Relevant to your Red Teaming: if you manage Windows servers, this limits immediate action but provides monitoring.)

Policies allow scheduling updates with configurable patch delays and host group assignments, enabling automated patching. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab/DevOps: reduces manual intervention and ensures consistent patching cycles.)
Host groups are managed under Settings, not under the Patching menu, which the author notes as unintuitive. (🟢 Relevant to your homelab: be aware of this UI quirk to avoid confusion.)
The tool is free and open-source, self-hosted, and supports multi-factor authentication. (🟢 Relevant to your security mindset: self-hosting gives full control, and MFA adds a layer of protection.)
methodology_steps: 1. Create a directory and navigate into it: 'mkdir patchmon && cd patchmon'.

2. Run the setup script to pull the Docker Compose and environment files: 'bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PatchMon/PatchMon/refs/heads/main/docker/setup-env.sh)"'. The script will ask if the installation will be behind a reverse proxy and for the domain name to configure CORS.
4. Access the web interface via the configured URL and complete the setup wizard: create an admin account, configure MFA, confirm the server URL (used by agents), and opt in/out of news.

5. Add hosts: navigate to Hosts > + Add Host, select OS type (Linux/FreeBSD/Windows), enter friendly name and host group, choose integrations (e.g., Docker), then copy and run the generated onboarding script on the target host.

6. To patch a host: click on the host, then 'Patch all'. In the wizard, select timing (default policy or override, e.g., 'Run immediately'), approve, and submit to queue. Monitor real-time shell output in the UI.
7. To automate patching: go to Patching > Policies > + Create policy, name it, set patch delay (schedule), and assign host groups (created under Settings > Host groups).
conclusions: PatchMon V2 effectively bridges the gap between visibility and action in patch management, allowing administrators to apply updates directly from a single dashboard without switching tools. This streamlines workflows and reduces the risk of missing critical patches.

The tool's support for Linux, FreeBSD, and Windows (with Windows patching expected later) makes it a versatile solution for heterogeneous environments, though Windows users currently only get monitoring.

Being free, open-source, and self-hosted, PatchMon V2 is an attractive option for home labs and production environments where cost and control are priorities. The Docker-based installation simplifies deployment and maintenance.

The author notes that host group management is located under Settings rather than Patching, which may cause initial confusion, but overall the interface is intuitive and the real-time patching output is a standout feature.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests as it introduces a free, open-source, self-hosted patch management tool that centralises updates across Linux, FreeBSD, and Windows servers. For your Red Teaming work, while PatchMon is a defensive tool, understanding its capabilities helps you anticipate how defenders might automate patching, and you could potentially test its security (e.g., API endpoints, agent communication). As a programmer, you can inspect the open-source code to customise or integrate it into your automation pipelines. The Docker Compose setup aligns with your DevOps skills, and the policy-based automation can reduce manual overhead in your lab.
extra_advice: Consider deploying PatchMon V2 in your homelab to automate patching across your Linux servers. As a Red Teamer, you could audit the tool's security by reviewing its GitHub repository for vulnerabilities (e.g., insecure agent registration, lack of input validation) and potentially use it as a target for testing lateral movement or privilege escalation scenarios. Additionally, since it's open-source, you could extend it to support custom patch repositories or integrate it with your existing monitoring stack (e.g., Prometheus/Grafana) via its API.
